title: Error Handling & Schedulers
description: Handle failures gracefully with catch, retry, and replaceError, and control execution context with Combine's scheduler API — receive(on:) and subscribe(on:) — for thread-safe reactive pipelines.

sections.content: Combine treats errors as first-class terminal events. When a publisher fails, the failure propagates downstream and the entire pipeline terminates — no more values will be delivered. This is by design: it forces you to handle errors explicitly rather than relying on try-catch blocks that are easy to forget in asynchronous code. The most common error-handling operator is catch, which replaces a failed publisher with a fallback publisher. For example, a network request that fails can be caught with a Just publisher that provides a default cached value, keeping the subscriber alive. The replaceError operator is simpler — it replaces any failure with a single value and finishes. If you want to retry a failing operation, use retry(n), which re-subscribes to the upstream up to n times before giving up. For more complex retry strategies with exponential backoff, you chain retry with a delay operator. The tryMap, tryFilter, and tryCompactMap operators let you throw errors from inside transforms — if the closure throws, the publisher fails with that error. This is how you bridge throwing Swift APIs into Combine pipelines. Always remember that once a catch replaces a failure, the resulting publisher's Failure type becomes Never — the pipeline is guaranteed not to fail after that point.

sections.title: catch & replaceError (Simulated)

sections.content: Schedulers control where pipeline events execute — which queue, which run loop, or which dispatch context. The two fundamental scheduler operators are subscribe(on:) and receive(on:). subscribe(on:) sets the execution context for the upstream publisher's work — the subscription, the value generation, and any side effects happen on the specified scheduler. receive(on:) sets the execution context for downstream operators and subscribers. A common pattern is subscribe(on: DispatchQueue.global()) to do heavy work on a background queue, then receive(on: DispatchQueue.main) to update UI on the main thread. The order matters: subscribe(on:) affects the upstream, receive(on:) affects the downstream from that point onward. You can have multiple receive(on:) calls in a pipeline to switch contexts mid-stream. Combine's built-in schedulers are DispatchQueue, RunLoop, and OperationQueue. DispatchQueue is the most common choice — use .global() for background work and .main for UI updates. The scheduler also affects timing operators: debounce, throttle, and delay all use the downstream scheduler's clock. When testing, you can inject ImmediateScheduler to make timing operators fire synchronously, eliminating flaky async waits in unit tests.

sections.title: Scheduler Context Switching (Simulated)

sections.question: After applying catch to a publisher that can fail with MyError, what does the resulting publisher's Failure type become?
sections.explanation: catch replaces a failed publisher with a fallback that cannot fail, so the resulting publisher's Failure type becomes Never. This guarantee propagates downstream, meaning no further catch is needed.

sections.question: What is the difference between subscribe(on:) and receive(on:)?
options: subscribe(on:) is for error handling; receive(on:) is for value handling
subscribe(on:) affects upstream work context; receive(on:) affects downstream delivery context
They are interchangeable — both set the same execution context
subscribe(on:) must be called last; receive(on:) must be called first
sections.explanation: subscribe(on:) determines where the upstream publisher does its work (subscription, value generation), while receive(on:) determines where downstream operators and subscribers receive values. A typical pattern is subscribe(on: .global()) followed by receive(on: .main).
